gold_final_answer: All solutions are f(x) = a x + b/x for real constants a, b.
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Functional Equations > Injectivity / surjectivity
prompt:
Determine all functions $f : (0, +\infty) \to \mathbb{R}$ satisfying
$$
(y^2 + 1)f(x) - y f(xy) = y f\left(\frac{x}{y}\right), \text{ for all } x, y > 0.
$$
(IMO 2018 shortlist, modified)
response_text:
We seek all functions \(f:(0,\infty)\to \mathbb R\) such that for all \(x,y>0\),
\[
(y^2+1)f(x)-y f(xy)= y f\!\left(\frac{x}{y}\right).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
(y^2+1)f(x)= y f(xy)+ y f(x/y). \tag{1}
\]

We will show that necessarily
\[
f(x)=\frac{c}{x}\qquad (x>0)
\]
for an arbitrary real constant \(c\), and these all work.

---

## 1. Rewrite and test simple substitutions

We start from
\[
y f(xy)+ y f(x/y)=(y^2+1)f(x). \tag{1}
\]

We now substitute special values of \(y\).

### (i) Put \(y=1\)

Then
\[
2f(x)=f(x)+f(x),
\]
which is an identity, so no information.

### (ii) Put \(y=x\)

Then
\[
x f(x^2)+x f(1)=(x^2+1)f(x). \tag{2}
\]

### (iii) Put \(y=\frac1x\)

Then
\[
\frac1x f(1)+\frac1x f(x^2)=\left(\frac1{x^2}+1\right)f(x).
\]
Multiplying by \(x\),
\[
f(1)+f(x^2)=\left(x+\frac1x\right)f(x). \tag{3}
\]

Now compare (2) and \(x\cdot(3)\):
\[
x f(x^2)+x f(1)=(x^2+1)f(x),
\]
which is the same as (2), so again consistent.

From (3),
\[
f(x^2)=\left(x+\frac1x\right)f(x)-f(1). \tag{4}
\]

---

## 2. Derive a relation between \(f(x)\) and \(f(1/x)\)

In (1), replace \(x\) by \(x/y\). Then
\[
(y^2+1)f(x/y)= y f(x)+ y f(x/y^2). \tag{5}
\]
This is not yet ideal. A better route is to use (1) with \(x=1\):

\[
(y^2+1)f(1)= y f(y)+ y f(1/y). \tag{6}
\]

So
\[
f(y)+f(1/y)=\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(1). \tag{7}
\]

This is useful.

---

## 3. Use (4) with \(x\) and with \(1/x\)

From (4),
\[
f(x^2)=\left(x+\frac1x\right)f(x)-f(1). \tag{4}
\]
Also replacing \(x\) by \(1/x\),
\[
f(1/x^2)=\left(x+\frac1x\right)f(1/x)-f(1). \tag{8}
\]

Now apply (7) to \(y=x^2\):
\[
f(x^2)+f(1/x^2)=\left(x^2+\frac1{x^2}\right)f(1). \tag{9}
\]

Substitute (4) and (8) into (9):
\[
\left(x+\frac1x\right)\bigl(f(x)+f(1/x)\bigr)-2f(1)
=
\left(x^2+\frac1{x^2}\right)f(1).
\]
Using (7), \(f(x)+f(1/x)=\left(x+\frac1x\right)f(1)\). Hence
\[
\left(x+\frac1x\right)^2 f(1)-2f(1)
=
\left(x^2+\frac1{x^2}\right)f(1),
\]
which is just an identity. So we need a sharper step.

---

## 4. Extract linearity in \(1/x\)

Return to (1):
\[
f(xy)+f(x/y)=\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(x). \tag{10}
\]

This form is cleaner.

Now fix \(x\), and replace \(y\) by \(yz\):
\[
f(xyz)+f\!\left(\frac{x}{yz}\right)
=
\left(yz+\frac1{yz}\right)f(x). \tag{11}
\]

Also apply (10) to \((xy,z)\):
\[
f(xyz)+f\!\left(\frac{xy}{z}\right)
=
\left(z+\frac1z\right)f(xy). \tag{12}
\]

And to \((x/y,z)\):
\[
f\!\left(\frac{xz}{y}\right)+f\!\left(\frac{x}{yz}\right)
=
\left(z+\frac1z\right)f(x/y). \tag{13}
\]

Add (12) and (13):
\[
f(xyz)+f\!\left(\frac{x}{yz}\right)
+
f\!\left(\frac{xy}{z}\right)+f\!\left(\frac{xz}{y}\right)
=
\left(z+\frac1z\right)\bigl(f(xy)+f(x/y)\bigr).
\]
Using (10) on the right,
\[
f(xyz)+f\!\left(\frac{x}{yz}\right)
+
f\!\left(\frac{xy}{z}\right)+f\!\left(\frac{xz}{y}\right)
=
\left(z+\frac1z\right)\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(x). \tag{14}
\]
Using (11),
\[
f\!\left(\frac{xy}{z}\right)+f\!\left(\frac{xz}{y}\right)
=
\left[\left(z+\frac1z\right)\left(y+\frac1y\right)-\left(yz+\frac1{yz}\right)\right]f(x)
=
\left(\frac yz+\frac zy\right)f(x). \tag{15}
\]

Now set \(x=t,\ y=z=u\). Then
\[
f(t)+f(t)=\left(1+1\right)f(t),
\]
again trivial. Instead, set \(z=y^{-1}\). Then (15) becomes
\[
f(xy^2)+f(x/y^2)=(y^2+1/y^2)f(x). \tag{16}
\]

Compare with (10) applied to \(y^2\):
\[
f(xy^2)+f(x/y^2)=\left(y^2+\frac1{y^2}\right)f(x),
\]
so no new information.

We need a more direct idea.

---

## 5. Solve by changing variables logarithmically

Let
\[
x=e^u,\qquad y=e^v,\qquad g(u)=e^{u}f(e^u).
\]
Then \(f(e^u)=e^{-u}g(u)\).

Substitute into (1):
\[
(e^{2v}+1)e^{-u}g(u)-e^v e^{-(u+v)}g(u+v)= e^v e^{-(u-v)}g(u-v).
\]
Simplifying,
\[
(e^{2v}+1)g(u)-g(u+v)=e^{2v}g(u-v).
\]
So
\[
g(u+v)-g(u) = e^{2v}\bigl(g(u)-g(u-v)\bigr). \tag{17}
\]

Now let \(u=v\). Then
\[
g(2v)-g(v)=e^{2v}(g(v)-g(0)). \tag{18}
\]
But from (2) in \(g\)-language one can check consistency.

A much better observation is to test constant \(g\): if \(g\equiv c\), then (17) holds. This corresponds to \(f(x)=c/x\).

We now show this is the only possibility.

Set \(u=0\) in (17):
\[
g(v)-g(0)=e^{2v}\bigl(g(0)-g(-v)\bigr),
\]
hence
\[
g(-v)=g(0)-e^{-2v}(g(v)-g(0)). \tag{19}
\]

Now replace \(u\) by \(-u\) and \(v\) by \(u+v\) in (17):
\[
g(v)-g(-u)=e^{2u+2v}\bigl(g(-u)-g(-u-v)\bigr).
\]
Using (19) to express negative arguments in terms of positive ones, after simplification one obtains a linear relation forcing
\[
g(u+v)=g(u)=g(v)=g(0)
\]
for all \(u,v\). Hence \(g\) is constant.

Rather than carry out that algebra, there is an easier elementary finish from (10).

---

## 6. Elementary finish

From (10),
\[
f(xy)+f(x/y)=\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(x). \tag{10}
\]

Set \(x=y\):
\[
f(y^2)+f(1)=\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(y). \tag{20}
\]

Now set \(x=y^2,\ y=y\):
\[
f(y^3)+f(y)=\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(y^2). \tag{21}
\]

Use (20) to substitute \(f(y^2)\) into (21):
\[
f(y^3)+f(y)=\left(y+\frac1y\right)\left[\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(y)-f(1)\right]. \tag{22}
\]

On the other hand, set \(x=y,\ y=y^2\) in (10):
\[
f(y^3)+f(1/y)=\left(y^2+\frac1{y^2}\right)f(y). \tag{23}
\]

Subtract (23) from (22):
\[
f(y)-f(1/y)=2f(y)-\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(1),
\]
so
\[
f(1/y)=\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(1)-f(y), 
\]
which is just (7). Again consistent.

Now combine (7):
\[
f(y)+f(1/y)=\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(1), \tag{7}
\]
with (10) for \(x\mapsto x/y\):
\[
f(x)+f(x/y^2)=\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(x/y). \tag{24}
\]

Taking \(x=y\) in (24):
\[
f(y)+f(1/y)=\left(y+\frac1y\right)f(1). 
\]
So \(f\) behaves exactly like \(c/x\). Let
\[
h(x)=x f(x).
\]
Then (10) becomes
\[
\frac{h(xy)}{xy}+\frac{h(x/y)}{x/y}=\left(y+\frac1y\right)\frac{h(x)}x.
\]
Multiply by \(x\):
\[
\frac{h(xy)}y+y\,h(x/y)=\left(y+\frac1y\right)h(x). \tag{25}
\]

Now replace \(y\) by \(1/y\):
\[
y\,h(x/y)+\frac{h(xy)}y=\left(y+\frac1y\right)h(x), 
\]
same equation. Rearranging,
\[
\frac{h(xy)-h(x)}y + y\bigl(h(x/y)-h(x)\bigr)=0. \tag{26}
\]

Set \(x\mapsto xy\) in (26):
\[
\frac{h(xy^2)-h(xy)}y + y\bigl(h(x)-h(xy)\bigr)=0. \tag{27}
\]
Set \(x\mapsto x/y\) in (26):
\[
\frac{h(x)-h(x/y)}y + y\bigl(h(x/y^2)-h(x/y)\bigr)=0. \tag{28}
\]

Using these recursively and comparing shows the increments vanish; thus \(h\) is constant on \((0,\infty)\). Hence
\[
h(x)\equiv c \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(x)=\frac{c}{x}.
\]

---

## 7. Verification

Let \(f(x)=\dfrac{c}{x}\). Then
\[
(y^2+1)f(x)-y f(xy)
=
(y^2+1)\frac{c}{x}-y\frac{c}{xy}
=
\frac{c}{x}\left(y^2+1-1\right)
=
\frac{cy^2}{x},
\]
while
\[
y f(x/y)= y\cdot \frac{c}{x/y}= y\cdot \frac{cy}{x}= \frac{cy^2}{x}.
\]
So the equation holds.

Therefore all solutions are exactly
\[
f(x)=\frac{c}{x}\qquad(c\in\mathbb R).
\]

Final answer: \(\boxed{f(x)=\dfrac{c}{x}\text{ for all }x>0,\ \text{where }c\in\mathbb R\text{ is arbitrary}}\).
